function_explanation: Computes the Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio (PSNR) between a reference image and a test image.
function_docstring:
Compute Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio between two arrays.

PSNR is a quality metric commonly used to measure the reconstruction quality
of lossy compression or image processing algorithms. Higher values indicate
better quality (less difference between reference and test).

Args:
    ref: Reference array (e.g., original image). Can be any numeric numpy array
        or array-like structure. Will be cast to float64 for computation.
    test: Test array (e.g., reconstructed/processed image) to compare against
        the reference. Must have the same shape as ref. Will be cast to float64
        for computation.
    data_range: The dynamic range of the input data (difference between maximum
        and minimum possible values). If None, it is computed from the reference
        array as (ref.max() - ref.min()). Defaults to None.

Returns:
    float: The PSNR value in decibels (dB). Returns 100.0 if the mean squared
        error is essentially zero (MSE < 1e-30), indicating identical arrays.
usage_example:
import numpy as np
from agent_compute_psnr import compute_psnr

# Create reference and test images
ref_image = np.random.rand(256, 256) * 255
# Add some noise to create test image
test_image = ref_image + np.random.randn(256, 256) * 10

# Compute PSNR with auto-detected data range
psnr_auto = compute_psnr(ref_image, test_image)
print(f"PSNR (auto range): {psnr_auto:.2f} dB")

# Compute PSNR with explicit data range (e.g., for 8-bit images)
psnr_explicit = compute_psnr(ref_image, test_image, data_range=255)
print(f"PSNR (explicit range): {psnr_explicit:.2f} dB")
